You cannot disable spanning tree on some switches and bridges in a VLAN and leave it enabled on other switches and bridges in the same VLAN because switches and bridges with spanning tree enabled have incomplete information about the physical topology of the network.  • We do not recommend disabling spanning tree, even in a topology that is free of physical loops.

name: switchport
detail: Put an interface that is in Layer 3 mode into Layer 2 mode for Layer 2 configuration.
usageSummary: Use the no switchport command (without parameters) to set the interface to the routed-interface status and to erase all Layer 2 configurations.  You must use this command before assigning an IP address to a routed port.  Entering the no switchport command shuts the port down and then reenables it, which might generate messages on the device to which the port is connected.

name: switchport access vlan
detail: Configure a port as a static-access port.

params.name: vlan-id
params.description: VLAN ID of the access mode VLAN; the range is 1 to 4094.

usageSummary: The port must be in access mode before the switchport access vlan command can take effect.  If the switchport mode is set to access vlan vlan-id, the port operates as a member of the specified VLAN.  An access port can be assigned to only one VLAN.

name: switchport dot1q ethertype
detail: Specify the EtherType value to be programmed on the interface.

params.name: value
params.description: EtherType value for 802.1Q encapsulation; valid values are from 0x600 to 0xFFFF.

usageSummary: You can configure a custom EtherType-field value on trunk ports and on access ports.  Each port supports only one EtherType-field value.  A port that is configured with a custom EtherType-field value does not recognize frames that have any other EtherType-field value as tagged frames.

name: switchport mode
detail: Configure the VLAN membership mode of a port.
syntax: switchport mode {access | dynamic | {auto | desirable} | trunk} noswitchport mode {access | dynamic | {auto | desirable} | trunk}

params.name: access
params.description: Sets the port to access mode (either static-access or dynamic-access depending on the setting of the switchport access vlan interface configuration command). The port is set to access unconditionally and operates as a nontrunking, single VLAN interface that sends and receives nonencapsulated (non-tagged) frames. An access port can be assigned to only one VLAN.

params.name: dynamic auto
params.description: Sets the port trunking mode dynamic parameter to auto to specify that the interface convert the link to a trunk link. This is the default switchport mode.

params.name: dynamic
params.description: Sets the port trunking mode dynamic parameter to desirable to specify that the interface

params.name: desirable
params.description: actively attempt to convert the link to a trunk link.

params.name: trunk
params.description: Sets the port to trunk unconditionally. The port is a trunking VLAN Layer 2 interface. The port sends and receives encapsulated (tagged) frames that identify the VLAN of origination. A trunk is a point-to-point link between two switches or between a switch and a router.

usageSummary: A configuration that uses the access,or trunk keywords takes effect only when you configure the port in the appropriate mode by using the switchport mode command.  The static-access and trunk configuration are saved, but only one configuration is active at a time.  When you enter access mode, the interface changes to permanent nontrunking mode and negotiates to convert the link into a nontrunk link even if the neighboring interface does not agree to the change.

name: switchport nonegotiate
detail: Specify that Dynamic Trunking Protocol (DTP) negotiation packets are not sent on the Layer 2 interface.
usageSummary: The no switchport nonegotiate command removes nonegotiate status.  This command is valid only when the interface switchport mode is access or trunk (configured by using the switchport mode access or the switchport mode trunk interface configuration command).  This command returns an error if you attempt to execute it in dynamic (auto or desirable) mode.

name: switchport trunk
detail: Set the trunk characteristics when the interface is in trunking mode.
syntax: Note The keyword native vlan tag is supported only on Cisco Catalyst 9500 Series Switches - High Performance. switchport trunk {allowed vlan vlan-list | native vlan {tag |vlan-id} | pruning vlan vlan-list}
noForm: no switchport trunk {allowed vlan | native vlan[tag] | pruning vlan}

params.name: native vlan vlan-id
params.description: Sets the native VLAN for sending and receiving untagged traffic when the interface is in IEEE 802.1Q trunking mode. The range is 1 to 4094.

params.name: native vlan tag
params.description: Enables native VLAN tagging on a particular trunk port. pruning vlan vlan-list Sets the list of VLANs that are eligible for VTP pruning when in trunking mode. See the Usage Guidelines for the vlan-list choices.

usageSummary: The vlan-list format is all | none | [add | remove | except] vlan-atom [,vlan-atom... ]: • all specifies all VLANs from 1 to 4094.  This is the default.

name: switchport voice vlan
detail: Configure voice VLAN on the port.
syntax: switchport voice vlan {vlan-id | dot1p | none | untagged | name vlan_name}

params.name: vlan-id
params.description: The VLAN to be used for voice traffic. The range is 1 to 4094. By default, the IP phone forwards the voice traffic with an IEEE 802.1Q priority of 5.

params.name: dot1p
params.description: Configures the telephone to use IEEE 802.1p priority tagging and uses VLAN 0 (the native VLAN). By default, the Cisco IP phone forwards the voice traffic with an IEEE 802.1p priority of 5.

params.name: none
params.description: Does not instruct the IP telephone about the voice VLAN. The telephone uses the configuration from the telephone key pad.

params.name: untagged
params.description: Configures the telephone to send untagged voice traffic. This is the default for the telephone. name vlan_name (Optional) Specifies the VLAN name to be used for voice traffic. You can enter up to 128 characters.

usageSummary: You should configure voice VLAN on Layer 2 access ports.  You must enable Cisco Discovery Protocol (CDP) on the switch port connected to the Cisco IP phone for the device to send configuration information to the phone.  CDP is enabled by default globally and on the interface.

name: transport unicast ipv4 local loopback
detail: Configure a unicast IPv4 connection from a loopback interface.
syntax: transport unicast ipv4 local loopback value

params.name: value
params.description: Loopback interface number. The maximum number of sessions supported is 127.

usageSummary: Configure the PTP property name using the ptp property command before configuring a unicast IPv4 connection from a loopback interface.

name: udld
detail: Enable aggressive or normal mode in the UniDirectional Link Detection (UDLD) and to set the configurable message timer time.
syntax: udld {aggressive | enable | fast-hello error-reporting | message time message-timer-interval | recovery interval recovery-timer-interval}

params.name: aggressive
params.description: Enables UDLD in aggressive mode on all fiber-optic interfaces.

params.name: enable
params.description: Enables UDLD in normal mode on all fiber-optic interfaces.

params.name: fast-hello error-reporting
params.description: Reports link failure on the console instead of err-disabling the affected Fast UDLD port.

params.name: message time
params.description: Configures the period of time between UDLD probe messages on

params.name: message-timer-interval
params.description: ports that are in the advertisement phase and are determined to be bidirectional. The range is 1 to 90 seconds. The default is 15 seconds.

params.name: recovery interval
params.description: Configures the error disable recovery timer value. recovery-timer-interval

usageSummary: UDLD supports two modes of operation: normal (the default) and aggressive.  In normal mode, UDLD detects unidirectional links due to misconnected interfaces on fiber-optic connections.  In aggressive mode, UDLD also detects unidirectional links due to one-way traffic on fiber-optic and twisted-pair links and due to misconnected interfaces on fiber-optic links.

name: udld fast-hello
detail: Enable Fast UniDirectional Link Detection (UDLD) on an individual interface which has UDLD configured on it.

params.name: message-timer-interval
params.description: Configures time in milliseconds between sending of messages in steady state. The range is from 200 to 1000 milliseconds.

usageSummary: A UDLD-capable port cannot detect a unidirectional link if it is connected to a UDLD-incapable port of another device.  UDLD supports two modes of operation: normal (the default) and aggressive.  In normal mode, UDLD detects unidirectional links due to misconnected interfaces on fiber-optic connections.

name: udld port
detail: Enable UniDirectional Link Detection (UDLD) on an individual interface or to prevent a fiber-optic interface from being enabled by the udld command in global configuration mode.

params.name: aggressive
params.description: (Optional) Enables UDLD in aggressive mode on the specified interface.

params.name: disable
params.description: (Optional) Disables UDLD on the specified interface despite the global UDLD configuration.

usageSummary: A UDLD-capable port cannot detect a unidirectional link if it is connected to a UDLD-incapable port of another device.  UDLD supports two modes of operation: normal (the default) and aggressive.  In normal mode, UDLD detects unidirectional links due to misconnected interfaces on fiber-optic connections.

name: udld reset
detail: Reset all interfaces disabled by UniDirectional Link Detection (UDLD) and permit traffic to begin passing through them again (though other features, such as spanning tree, Port Aggregation Protocol (PAgP), and Dynamic Trunking Protocol (DTP) still have their normal effects, if enabled).
usageSummary: If the interface configuration is still enabled for UDLD, these ports begin to run UDLD again and are disabled for the same reason if the problem has not been corrected.  This example shows how to reset all interfaces disabled by UDLD: Device> enable Device# udld reset 1 ports shutdown by UDLD were reset.  Command Reference, Cisco IOS XE 17.

name: vlan dot1q tag native
detail: Enable tagging of native VLAN frames on all IEEE 802.1Q trunk ports.
usageSummary: When enabled, native VLAN packets going out of all IEEE 802. 1Q trunk ports are tagged.  When disabled, native VLAN packets going out of all IEEE 802.

name: vtp mode
detail: Configure the VLAN Trunking Protocol (VTP) device mode, use thevtp mode command. To revert to the default server mode.

params.name: client
params.description: Specifies the device as a client.

params.name: off
params.description: Specifies the device mode as off.

params.name: server
params.description: Specifies the device as a server.

params.name: transparent
params.description: Specifies the device mode as transparent.

modes: Usage Guidelines VLAN Trunking Protocol (VTP) is a Cisco Proprietary Layer 2 messaging protocol used to distribute the VLAN configuration information across multiple devices within a VTP domain. Without VTP, you must configure VLANs in each device in the network. Using VTP, you configure VLANs on a VTP server and then distribute the configuration to other VTP devices in the VTP domain. In VTP transparent mode, you can configure VLANs (add, delete, or modify) and private VLANs. VTP transparent switches do not participate in VTP. A VTP transparent switch does not advertise its VLAN configuration and does not synchronize its VLAN configuration based on received advertisements. The VTP configuration revision number is always set to zero (0). Transparent switches do forward VTP advertisements that they receive out their trunk ports in VTP version 2. A VTP device mode can be one of the following: • server —You can create, modify, and delete VLANs and specify other configuration parameters, such as VTP version, for the entire VTP domain. VTP servers advertise their VLAN configuration to other switches in the same VTP domain and synchronize their VLAN configuration with other switches based on advertisements received over trunk links. VTP server is the default mode.
Note You can configure VLANs 1 to 1005. VLANs 1002 to 1005 are reserved for token ring in VTP version 2.
• client —VTP clients behave the same way as VTP servers, but you cannot create, change, or delete VLANs on a VTP client.
Command Reference, Cisco IOS XE 17.18.x (Catalyst 9500 Switches) 1107 Layer 2/3 vtp mode

• transparent —You can configure VLANs (add, delete, or modify) and private VLANs. VTP transparent switches do not participate in VTP. A VTP transparent switch does not advertise its VLAN configuration and does not synchronize its VLAN configuration based on received advertisements. Because of this, the VTP configuration revision number is always set to zero (0). Transparent switches do forward VTP advertisements that they receive out their trunk ports in VTP version 2. • off —In the above three described modes, VTP advertisements are received and transmitted as soon as the switch enters the management domain state. In the VTP off mode, switches behave the same as in VTP transparent mode with the exception that VTP advertisements are not forwarded. You can use this VTP device to monitor the VLANs.
Note If you use the no vtp mode command to remove a VTP device, the device will be configured as a VTP server. Use the vtp mode off command to remove a VTP device.
Example This example shows how to configure a VTP device in transparent mode and add VLANs 2, 3, and 4: Device> enable Device(config)#vtp mode transparent Device(config)# vlan 2-4
Example This example shows how to remove a device configured as a VTP device: Device> enable Device(config)# vtp mode off
Example This example shows how to configure a VTP device as a VTP server and adds VLANs 2 and 3: Device> enable Device# vtp mode server Device(config)# vlan 2,3
Example This example shows how to configure a VTP device as a client: Device> enable Device# vtp mode client

usageSummary: VLAN Trunking Protocol (VTP) is a Cisco Proprietary Layer 2 messaging protocol used to distribute the VLAN configuration information across multiple devices within a VTP domain.  Without VTP, you must configure VLANs in each device in the network.  Using VTP, you configure VLANs on a VTP server and then distribute the configuration to other VTP devices in the VTP domain.
